disease.name: Obstetric Emergencies in Intensive Care

emergencyPriority.notes: Obstetric emergencies are life-threatening conditions with dual maternal-fetal risk. Requires multidisciplinary team with maternal-fetal medicine, critical care, and neonatology. Cesarean delivery within 5 minutes may be required.

mustNotMiss: Amniotic fluid embolism: sudden cardiovascular collapse, DIC, hypoxia during labor or cesarean section
Eclampsia: new-onset grand mal seizures in a preeclamptic patient (may occur antepartum, intrapartum, or up to 6 weeks postpartum)
Peripartum cardiomyopathy: heart failure in last month of pregnancy through 5 months postpartum
HELLP syndrome: hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, low platelets (complication of severe preeclampsia)
Obstetric hemorrhage: leading cause of maternal mortality worldwide; massive transfusion protocol activation critical
Uterine rupture: complete disruption of uterine wall, fetal distress, maternal shock
Septic abortion or chorioamnionitis with septic shock
diagnosticClues: Eclampsia: generalized tonic-clonic seizures without other cause in preeclamptic patient; BP typically > 160/110, proteinuria, hyperreflexia
HELLP: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes, low haptoglobin), AST > 70 U/L, platelet count < 100,000/uL
Amniotic fluid embolism: acute hypoxemia, hypotension, DIC, altered mental status during labor or shortly after delivery
Peripartum cardiomyopathy: LVEF < 45% by echocardiography, onset in peripartum period, no other identifiable cause of heart failure
Obstetric hemorrhage: estimated blood loss > 1000 mL for cesarean, > 500 mL for vaginal delivery; vital sign changes are late signs
exclusionClues: Normal BP and absence of proteinuria effectively excludes preeclampsia-eclampsia
Normal platelet count and liver enzymes exclude HELLP syndrome
Improved oxygenation and hemodynamics after delivery argues against AFE (which typically worsens)
Negative cardiac MRI for myocarditis and normal coronary angiography helps differentiate peripartum cardiomyopathy from other causes
Absence of fundal tenderness, normal fetal heart tracing, and low index of suspicion makes uterine rupture unlikely

tools.name: Maternal Early Warning Criteria (MEWC)
tools.interpretation: Any single criterion triggers escalation. Combines modified early obstetric warning system (MEOWS) and UK MBRRACE-LE recommendations. Primary screening tool for impending maternal deterioration.

tools.name: Preeclampsia Severity
classification: Severe: BP > 160/110, platelets < 100K, Cr > 1.1, AST > 2x normal, persistent headache/visual changes, epigastric pain, pulmonary edema
Mild: BP > 140/90 but < 160/110, mild proteinuria, no severe features

historyFeatures: Gestational age: preeclampsia typically after 20 weeks; peripartum cardiomyopathy last month to 5 months postpartum
Previous obstetric history: prior preeclampsia (recurrence risk 20-40%), prior HELLP, prior postpartum hemorrhage
Prenatal care: number of visits, BP trends, proteinuria, fetal growth assessments
Mode of delivery: vaginal vs cesarean, induction/augmentation, operative vaginal delivery
Total duration of ruptured membranes (chorioamnionitis risk)
Comorbidities: chronic hypertension, diabetes, obesity, advanced maternal age, multiple gestation, connective tissue disease
examFeatures: Vital signs: BP trend, tachycardia, tachypnea, oxygen saturation pending
Cardiovascular: JVD, S3 gallop, displaced PMI, lower extremity edema (peripartum cardiomyopathy)
Respiratory: crackles, wheezing, tachypnea (pulmonary edema in preeclampsia, cardiomyopathy)
Abdominal: fundal height, uterine tenderness, uterine tone (Couvelaire uterus in abruption), rebound/epigastric tenderness (HELLP hepatic capsule distension)
Neurologic: hyperreflexia, clonus (3 beats = abnormal), visual scotomata, twitching (impending eclampsia)
Pelvic: vaginal bleeding, degree of cervical dilation, fetal station
riskFactors: Chronic hypertension (most common risk factor for preeclampsia)
Nulliparity, multiple gestation, advanced maternal age (> 35), obesity (BMI > 30)
African American race (higher rates of preeclampsia, peripartum cardiomyopathy, and maternal mortality)

subtypes.name: Preeclampsia-Eclampsia Spectrum
subtypes.pathophysiology: Placental ischemia/hypoxia causes release of anti-angiogenic factors (sFlt-1, sEng) into maternal circulation, leading to endothelial dysfunction, hypertension, proteinuria, and in severe cases, seizures (eclampsia)

subtypes.name: Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Fetal material (amniotic fluid, fetal cells, lanugo) enters maternal pulmonary circulation causing anaphylactic-type reaction, pulmonary vasospasm, right heart failure, and DIC

subtypes.name: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy (PPCM)
subtypes.pathophysiology: Multifactorial: 16 kDa prolactin fragment (vasculotoxic), oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, myocarditis. VEGF inhibition from anti-angiogenic state contributes.

subtypes.name: Obstetric Hemorrhage
subtypes.pathophysiology: Uterine atony (most common, 70%), retained products of conception, genital tract laceration, uterine rupture, placental abruption, placenta previa/accreta, coagulopathy

mimics: Grand mal seizure from other causes in pregnancy (epilepsy, intracranial bleed, cerebral sinus thrombosis, reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome)
Pulmonary embolism vs AFE (both cause hypoxia and hypotension; AFE has more severe DIC)
Septic shock from chorioamnionitis or pyelonephritis vs AFE vs hemorrhage (often present simultaneously)
Cardiomyopathy: dilated vs peripartum vs postpartum viral myocarditis vs preexisting valvular disease
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy vs HELLP (AFLP has hypoglycemia, severe coagulopathy, microvesicular steatosis on biopsy)
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura vs HELLP (TTP has more severe thrombocytopenia, more schistocytes, ADAMTS13 deficiency)

investigations.test: Complete Blood Count with Platelets and Peripheral Smear
investigations.detail: Platelet count < 100K suggests HELLP. Schistocytes on smear = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Hemoglobin drop quantifies hemorrhage. Leukocytosis may be normal in pregnancy but suggests infection.

investigations.test: Liver Function Tests and Lactate Dehydrogenase
investigations.detail: AST/ALT > 70 U/L suggests liver involvement in HELLP. LDH > 600 U/L indicates hemolysis. Epigastric/RUQ pain with AST > 70 is a severe feature of preeclampsia.
PT/PTT/INR, fibrinogen (< 200 mg/dL suggests DIC in pregnancy), D-dimer. DIC complicates abruption, AFE, HELLP, and massive hemorrhage. Pregnancy normally elevates fibrinogen and factors.
Essential for suspected PPCM: LVEF < 45%, LV dilation, global hypokinesis. Assess for valvular disease, pulmonary hypertension, RV function. TEE if TTE inadequate.
Assess for pulmonary edema (cardiogenic vs non-cardiogenic), ARDS, pleural effusion. ALI/ARDS can complicate preeclampsia, AFE, chorioamnionitis.
Continuous fetal monitoring when fetus is viable (typically > 23-24 weeks). Late decelerations, minimal variability indicate fetal compromise and need for delivery.

pathophysiology.summary: Pregnancy creates a unique physiologic state of high cardiac output, low systemic vascular resistance, and hypercoagulability. Obstetric emergencies exploit vulnerabilities in this adaptation: placental ischemia triggers endothelial dysfunction (preeclampsia), fetal material enters maternal circulation inducing anaphylaxis and DIC (AFE), and the hemodynamic stress of pregnancy precipitates heart failure in susceptible women (PPCM).
mechanisms: Preeclampsia: placental hypoxia upregulates sFlt-1 which binds VEGF and PlGF, causing endothelial dysfunction, HTN, and proteinuria
HELLP: microvascular endothelial damage in liver sinusoids leads to fibrin deposition, hepatic necrosis, and thrombocytopenia
AFE: fetal antigens trigger complement activation, mast cell degranulation, pulmonary vasospasm, and consumptive coagulopathy
PPCM: 16-kDa prolactin fragment induces endothelial damage and cardiomyocyte death via cathepsin D cleavage

complications: Maternal death: obstetric hemorrhage and hypertensive disorders are leading causes worldwide
Fetal death or hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy from maternal cardiopulmonary instability
Massive transfusion complications: hypothermia, acidosis, hypocalcemia, abo incompatibility
Postpartum hemorrhage requiring hysterectomy (loss of fertility)
Renal failure: acute tubular necrosis from hypoperfusion, acute cortical necrosis from abruption

management.detail: Immediate: magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV bolus over 15-20 min, then 1-2 g/hr infusion for 24 hours. Recurrent seizures: additional 2 g IV bolus. BP control with labetalol 20 mg IV, then 40-80 mg q10 min (max 300 mg) or hydralazine 5-10 mg IV q20 min. Definitive treatment: delivery when maternal and fetal stabilized. Do NOT use diazepam or phenytoin (inferior to magnesium for eclampsia). Per ACOG Practice Bulletin 222.

Delivery is definitive treatment. Corticosteroids: dexamethasone 10 mg IV q12h x 2 doses for fetal lung maturity if < 34 weeks (controversial for improving maternal platelet count). BP control. Seizure prophylaxis with magnesium sulfate. Platelet transfusion if < 20K or < 50K with active bleeding. Treat DIC with supportive care. Per ACOG guidelines.

Supportive care: 100% O2, intubation for hypoxemia, vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) for hypotension. Treat DIC with massive transfusion protocol: PRBC:FFP:platelets in 1:1:1 ratio. Consider pulmonary vasodilators for severe right heart failure (inhaled NO, epoprostenol). Uterotonics for hemorrhage (oxytocin, methergine, carboprost, misoprostol). ECMO may be life-saving in refractory cases. High-quality CPR if cardiac arrest; perimortem cesarean delivery within 5 minutes if > 20 weeks.

Goal-directed medical therapy for heart failure: furosemide for congestion, hydralazine/nitrates instead of ACEi/ARB during pregnancy (ACEi/ARB safe postpartum), beta-blockers (metoprolol or carvedilol), spironolactone postpartum. Bromocriptine (2.5-5 mg BID) to suppress prolactin may improve recovery (based on small RCTs). Anticoagulation if LVEF < 35% due to high thromboembolism risk. LVAD or transplant for refractory cases. Per ESC Heart Failure Association guidelines.

Activate massive transfusion protocol early. Initial: 2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid restricted (hemodilution worsens coagulopathy). Transfuse PRBC:FFP:platelets 1:1:1 ratio. Goal: Hb > 7, platelets > 50K, fibrinogen > 150-200, pH > 7.2, ionized Ca > 1.1. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset (WOMAN trial, 2017). Uterotonics: oxytocin 10-40 U in 1L NS infusion, methergine 0.2 mg IM (contraindicated in HTN), carboprost 250 ug IM q15 min (asthma caution), misoprostol 600-1000 ug PR/SL. Surgical: intrauterine balloon, B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation/embolization, hysterectomy.

Vital sign monitoring frequency: q1h during active labor, q2h postpartum x 6h, then q4h if stable. Modified MEWC triggers: SBP < 90 or > 160, HR < 50 or > 120, RR < 10 or > 30, O2 sat < 95% on RA, oliguria < 35 mL/hr x 2h, altered mental status, chest pain, shortness of breath. Any trigger activates rapid response team including MFM and critical care. Based on UK MBRRACE-LE confidential enquiry recommendations.

Core team: MFM specialist, intensivist, anesthesiologist, neonatologist, blood bank, OR team. Pre-delivery huddle for high-risk patients. In situ simulation drills improve outcomes. Debriefing after critical events. Communication tools: SBAR for handoffs, closed-loop communication during crises. Per SMFM and SCCM joint guidelines for maternal critical care.
